questions.question: What are the four layers of the box model from inside to outside?
questions.explanation: The box model layers from inside out: Content → Padding → Border → Margin.

questions.question: What does box-sizing: border-box do?
options: Includes padding and border in the specified width
questions.explanation: border-box makes the specified width include content, padding, and border — total width = specified width + margin.

questions.question: If an element has width: 200px, padding: 20px, border: 5px, what is total width?
questions.explanation: 200 (content) + 20+20 (padding) + 5+5 (border) = 250px total width with content-box.

questions.question: Which layer of the box model is always transparent?
questions.explanation: Margin is always transparent and does not show background color. It creates space outside the border.

questions.question: What is the default box-sizing value in CSS?
questions.explanation: The default box-sizing is content-box, where the specified width/height only applies to the content area.

questions.question: In the box model, which layer's background color is visible?
questions.explanation: The background color extends into the padding area (and border if border is transparent), but not into margins.

questions.question: What tool is best for inspecting the box model of an element?
questions.explanation: Browser DevTools provide a visual box model diagram showing content, padding, border, and margin dimensions.

questions.question: With box-sizing: border-box, if width is 300px, border is 10px, and padding is 20px, what is content width?
questions.explanation: 300 - 10-10 (border) - 20-20 (padding) = 240px content width with border-box.

questions.question: Which CSS property determines whether an element can have width and height set?
questions.explanation: The display property determines box behavior. Block and inline-block elements respect width/height; inline elements don't.

questions.question: What happens to the box model when display: flex is applied?
options: The box model no longer applies
Flex items still follow the box model, but with different default behavior
questions.explanation: Flex items still have content, padding, border, and margin, but flex layout changes how margins are distributed.

questions.question: How does the box model affect element positioning?
options: Content, padding, border, and margin all contribute to the space an element occupies
questions.explanation: All four layers determine the total space an element occupies in the document flow.

questions.question: What is the difference between inline and block boxes?
options: Block boxes respect width/height; inline boxes don't
Inline boxes respect width/height; block boxes don't
They are identical in box behavior
questions.explanation: Block boxes fill available width and respect all box model properties. Inline boxes shrink to content width and ignore width/height.

questions.question: What does margin: 0 auto do in relation to the box model?
options: Centers a block element horizontally by distributing auto margins equally
Sets margins to 0 on all sides
questions.explanation: margin: 0 auto sets top/bottom to 0 and distributes remaining space equally on left/right, centering the element.

questions.question: What happens when two vertical margins meet in normal flow?
options: They collapse to the larger of the two values
questions.explanation: Vertical margins collapse in normal flow — the resulting margin equals the larger (not the sum) of the adjacent margins.

questions.question: Why is box-sizing: border-box considered easier for layout?
options: Width/height declarations include padding and border, making calculations simpler
It makes all elements the same size
It removes the need for margins
questions.explanation: With border-box, the specified width includes padding and border, so you don't need to mentally add them for total dimensions.

questions.question: Which DevTools panel shows the box model visualization?
questions.explanation: In the Elements panel, the Computed or Layout section shows a visual box model with current dimensions for each layer.

questions.question: What is content-box best used for?
options: When you need explicit control over content dimensions independent of padding/border
questions.explanation: content-box is useful when you need the content area to stay exactly the specified size regardless of padding or borders.

questions.question: How does padding affect background painting differently from margins?
options: Padding shows background; margins are transparent
Margins show background; padding is transparent
questions.explanation: The padding area inherits the element's background, while margins are always transparent.

questions.question: What is a common CSS reset for the box model?
questions.explanation: Applying box-sizing: border-box to all elements and pseudo-elements is the most common box-model reset pattern.

questions.question: Why is the box model considered the most important CSS concept?
options: It's the hardest concept to learn
Every element is rendered as a box, so understanding the model is foundational to all layout work
It determines which properties are valid
questions.explanation: Every element is rendered as a rectangular box, making the box model the foundation of all CSS layout work.
